sections.heading: hepatocellular_carcinoma
sections.content: The molecular pathogenesis of HCC is complex and typically involves multistep carcinogenesis within the context of chronic liver disease. Key genomic events include mutations or alterations in the TERT promoter (found in ~60% of HCC), which confers a survival advantage to hepatocytes. The Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway is frequently dysregulated, with activating mutations in CTNNB1 (β-catenin) occurring in ~30% of tumors. Other commonly mutated genes include TP53 (associated with aflatoxin B1 exposure and HBV infection), AXIN1, ARID1A, and NFE2L2. Chromosomal instability, including losses at 1p, 4q, 8p, 13q, and 16q, and gains at 1q and 8q, is common. Activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR and RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK pathways are critical drivers of proliferation and survival. The integration of viral DNA (HBV) into the host genome and the chronic inflammatory milieu (via cytokines like IL-6) create a pro-carcinogenic environment. [9]

sections.heading: biliary_tract_cancers
sections.content: Biliary tract cancers exhibit significant molecular heterogeneity based on anatomic subsite. For intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA), key driver mutations include IDH1 or IDH2 (in 10-20% of cases), leading to the production of the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate. FGFR2 gene fusions (e.g., FGFR2-BICC1) are found in approximately 10-15% of iCCA tumors and represent a actionable target. BRAF V600E mutations are present in ~5% of cases. For perihilar and distal cholangiocarcinomas, the molecular profile differs; they more closely resemble pancreatic adenocarcinoma, with a high frequency of KRAS mutations (20-30%), TP53 mutations, and SMAD4 alterations. Overexpression of the HER2 (ERBB2) oncogene is noted in a subset (10-20%) of gallbladder cancers. Chromatin remodeling gene mutations, such as ARID1A, ARID2, and PBRM1, are frequently identified across biliary cancer subtypes. Chronic inflammation is a unifying pathogenic driver, leading to DNA damage and activation of pro-tumorigenic pathways. [10,11]
